sentences.text: In Japan-- well, the way we lived is-- was a chapter by itself.
We didn't have enough for livelihoods.
It was all money that came from America-- I think from the American Joint Distribution-- to a committee in Japan.
And they gave us-- it was no kitchen-- to-- to-- they gave us some money.
And there was enough for-- and also a pound of rice bread again.
And very little money.
So we got a few-- we got a place where to sleep.
It was on the-- mattresses on the floors, Japanese style.
And we-- a few-- three or four guys always got together to cook together-- those little hibachi ovens.
[INAUDIBLE] And some how got a pot and we used to go out on the market and buy bananas that were put on the side, so had already black spots, and onions and noodles.
Noodles were very cheap there.
So we made all-- potatoes with noodles, made a soup.
And with a little oil-- oil was also very cheap.
And we have a meal every day like this, and this kept us.
And at night, we had some of these bananas that was, the fruit and vegetables.
And somehow there's nothing that I can think of that is out of the ordinary too much-- Do you remember--
--to talk about.
Of course, yes.
Those-- that-- that time-- In Japan, what I planned I was hoping still to get-- I was-- I-- the-- the men that I wrote-- wrote to America about helping me getting some dollars.
I got from him a notification that he sent me $100, and it's in-- in-- in some bureau in Tokyo.
So I-- going to Tokyo was too much money, and I didn't have it.
So I asked, but I didn't get to.
However, I-- all my mind was that there were still chances to send packages with food, or food products, or dry fruit to send-- be able to send home.
And this is what I-- I worked at it, and somehow it didn't work out.
But at least they got for me some mail knowing that I'm alive.

Well, in Japan, we knew all the news [? that ?] went [? down. ?] But there was the time-- yes, the big news was June the 22nd, where they started running around-- the boys with the papers-- that Russia attacked-- that German attacked Russia.
That was the big news then.
And then there-- there was an English paper in Kobe.
Some-- I didn't know any English but some.
The only ?] [INAUDIBLE] the news [INAUDIBLE] done.
And somehow we hoped then thinking that after they attacked Russia that they'll be broken so fast.
Soon enough, we found out how in the early stages, the [?
Russians-- ?] they got so many Russian prisoners in the thousand, ten thousand, and hundreds of thousand.
And we were there until then the Japanese decided they want to send us out.
Because we did not have an end visa-- where to go.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Very nice-- didn't bother us at all.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, very kind.
You know, if you need something.
You didn't know the language.
If you needed something, they draw you a map.
You ask for a street-- you show them this and this-- they draw you a map.
And one day I was-- it was very interesting-- one day I was asking for-- for directions.
And there's a woman with a man and didn't know-- they tried to throw me-- and it was raining.
They gave me the umbrella.
I should take the umbrella.
They had-- he had another umbrella.
And I had to take the umbrella and go-- go on.
They were very, very friendly.
But there was also-- the Japanese are a funny nation.
You know.
There were a few guys [? never ?] that didn't have enough money.
Probably ?] went to like a Macy's, and there was also a restaurant.
And they saw the way it goes.
They figured they ate some puddings-- they was very good.
And they-- some of them didn't pay [? and left. ?] It was, you know, so open you had to go over.
If you tried to sneak out, they probably-- they didn't do anything.
But a couple of guys did like this.
And somehow everybody thought oh, they got away with it.
[INAUDIBLE]
It was a little joke because everybody-- most of the people didn't have any money.
Sure enough, a day or two later the police were in [INAUDIBLE].
Here's the pictures of these people, and this and this is what they did.
So there was a warning out.
It never happened again.
But they were treating us very, very nicely.
But then around September-- before-- I mean, the whole summer-- we came there about in April sometimes-- the whole summer it was going pretty nicely.
But near the end, everybody has to leave to go to Shanghai.
They knew already that the war was gonna [INTERPOSING VOICES] breakout.
They-- they told us to go there because we didn't have any other place.
Shanghai was an open city.
So we-- and they had it already occupied.
But I didn't take the whole town because it was so-called an international city.
There was an English concession what's called Together with America that they controlled that area.
And there was a French concession-- a whole part of the town that was controlled by the French.
And than was a [INAUDIBLE] which was controlled by the Japanese.
Because there was no Chinese government in that area.
The capital of the Chinese government that was under Japanese control was Nanking.
And the-- the Communists was in the rest of the country.
So we-- everybody [INAUDIBLE] at least according to the alphabet-- earlier had to go earlier.
So I was on the list one day, and we went to Shanghai.
We had a boat-- a Japanese boat.
The Joint probably.
And we arrived to Shanghai.
It was a very short time before Yom-- Rosh Hashanah.
Started out from-- oh, it's-- is more.
Yeah.
This is-- this is already summer of '41, this is.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] Yeah.
And in Shanghai, I started a new life completely.
Shanghai there was starting a committee with a restaurant-- with a kitchen for us, which was also supported by the Joint for a while.
Not-- then-- then the Japan-- when Japan went in the war-- Japan wasn't in the war yet now.
When Germany attacked Russia the 22nd of June, Japan was still not in the war.
They sent us away because they knew they were gonna get in the war, apparently.
So in Shanghai we were for a while, and it was still American representative there, English, and everything.
Then all of a sudden, when Japan attacked Pearl Harbor, it was a new story completely.
They started to have control on us.
And they started-- they put us after a while-- they put us into a restricted area.
You had to have a sort of a passport.
Well, it's like a document to be able to go out to work.
You had to be-- go out a certain time according to your paper and come back in a certain time.
It was quite a little-- much different already-- more restricted but not direct [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] discrimination.
Because we Jews, they didn't want us to call it ghetto.
The Japanese were against it-- just a restricted area of some sort.
And we--
No, no, no-- Chinese people.
It was a big, big city with millions of-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
Well, this, there were also Chinese living there.
But they say up to here you can go and from here, you know, except with a permit.
We were there-- there was some people did some business, some people did some work.
There was-- and then we-- we got-- well at first, the yeshiva got.
We were giving some aids from the Polish consulate that was in the beginning together under the protection of the English.
But then there was no more, and some they came still, I think, through London from the Polish government as Polish citizens.
But then after a while, it was pretty hard because the Joint Distribution didn't have any direct route to send money from America because there was war between Japan and America.
So these-- the-- the same committee-- they were Russian Jews mostly in Shanghai-- the Russian Jews means those Jews that settled in Shanghai after the-- the Russian Revolution went away.
And they were already settled there.
Some are rich.
Some are middle class.
[INAUDIBLE].
So some of the rich people-- as we understood and heard-- lent money to the Joint Distribution committee secretly because Japan would not have liked it.
And this is somehow where the kitchen-- our kitchen still went on.
We still could eat the minimum, but and some-- there was some business to be made also.
Because-- What did you do all day?
What was your schedule like?
I didn't have a schedule.
We went once-- I remember that it was quite in the beginning.
We were looking for work.
So we asking, and finally somebody-- I don't remember-- came in that there's a Jewish manufacturer manufactured sodas-- you know, all kinds of beverages, sodas-- that he's looking for workers, OK?
We went there.
We worked so hard-- not only-- I never worked that hard.
A normal day's work for the hardest worker is too much.
Finally, the day's over and we're about to get paid.
And we got paid.
So with that money we were-- we had a-- Jewish refugees had a restaurant.
So in American money was very little.
To make the story short, that we went with that money together.
There wasn't enough to get one meal from the whole day work like a dog.
Of course, nobody went the next day no more.
That was it.
And then there was a little black market started.
Because as soon as there was war, there were some products that is usually imported-- some oil, sardines, coffee.
So if you knew your way around, you tried make here and there a little money.
So you went out on the street, and you met your palsy-walsys and talk about business and go here.
And you know an office here, maybe they have something there.
This was how the day went by somehow.
Some people at the yeshiva were sitting and learning.
Shabbos was all right.
Shabbos-- I think I was lucky enough.
Somehow I managed to be in that restaurant and eat in that restaurant Shabbos.
During the week was tough, didn't have much except that meal that we ate in the kitchen.
So bread was enough, and there was always a piece of meat.
There was-- We didn't starve.
We didn't-- I would say sometimes you may not have enough money.
But I didn't starve.
I mean, we weren't even hungry.
There was more just here and there.
If you need something-- to buy a shirt or for shoes-- it was very bad.
But we didn't-- in comparison to any part of the world that was-- had-- was under occupation had it worse.
And we were there until-- well, before the war ended.
The Americans came.
They bombed Shanghai.
When that happened we felt very bad because some of our people got killed.
They went-- like either they were drunk or they didn't give a hoot-- bombed there was no-- no-- no base.
No nothing.
There were bases that they never knew where they are.
There'd be soldiers with Japanese soldiers, army-- they didn't enjoy that.
The bombs fell-- one bomb, a small one, fell right in the, in the walk going to the kitchen.
That-- that day I missed lunch time.
The trolley cars went only a few-- an hour or two in the morning because they wanted to save electricity.
It was electricity-- at lunchtime back and forth and then in the evening.
So let's say 4:00 or 5:00.
So I went-- supposed to go at lunch.
We were only one car.
Lunch, that time, you go with a car, with a-- with a trolley car.
And I missed it with a minute.
I begged him to wait, didn't [INAUDIBLE] want to wait.
And that car-- the people that went with that car that was from work to go in to eat their lunch in our kitchen-- on the walk fell a bomb, a American bomb, a small one.
And a couple of people got killed.
Their lungs were torn from the pressure.
It didn't hit them.
One hit a little bit with [INAUDIBLE].
The pressure of-- because it fell very close-- the pressure removed their lung.
A few our people got killed.
A Mr.
Engel, a very nice person, [INAUDIBLE] got killed then-- a few people.
Yeah.
Sure.
But as far as-- not the little guys, but the big merchants were very, very correct in business.
Unbelievable.
Not the Japanese-- they were lying, and they were backing out.
Because after they surrendered-- the army after the atomic bomb-- they had-- the law was, the American law, that they had to run-- go back to Japan.
And they couldn't take only certain things, only money.
So they sold a lot of big pearls and-- the pearls especially-- a lot of things.
And we were doing-- buying [? their ?] businesses.
Oh, we were stupid enough not to understand the value of what is right and wrong.
But this is-- you always get smart later when you find out.
You can't be smart enough.
If you're lucky, you do the right things.
If you're not, you don't do the right things.
So they are cheaters, and-- and it's not a secret.
They're not ashamed of it.
I'm backing out, [? it'd ?] [? mean ?] something.
Not the-- the Chinese.
This is holy-- your word to them is so holy they wouldn't-- except some guys that they are bad people or a robber maybe-- I didn't come across.
I did some with offices-- you know, very correct.
It was unbelievable, unbelievably correct.
Very nice.
And after being there about five and a half years, I got the American visa.
Well, very simple.
That was very simple.
After atomic bomb-- the second atomic bomb-- Japan surrendered.
And as soon as Japan surrendered, they didn't bother us no more.
And we were on the-- we were-- everybody was drinking and dancing in the streets.
And the-- and then slowly, the American army arrived.
But first, came a few American officers that came by parachute or because they couldn't come yet with those mines.
Somehow they came.
I don't know.
And we saw and we talked.
And then came the army.
They settled there.
And somehow I had a cousin in Philadelphia who had not long organized a Bais Yaakov.
He sent me about just a paper.
I read the letterhead and saw that I am accepted as a professor for religious instructions in that school.
And I went up with it, and then the American consulate was very lenient.
I went up, and they gave me-- on the account of that, they gave me a visa.
Where'd I go?
To America.
We-- we landed in Tacoma, Washington as was then a strike on the Pacific Coast.
So there were only left a few boats that were already Shanghai.
They couldn't leave America then, but they were already there.
And they took, I think, silk, raw silk.
And somehow we got on their boat.
That's-- I think about four or five of our people they took.
Plus they got a little extra money.
It was a-- one of their-- a 6,000 ton boat from this-- no-- what was his name that built during the war so many ships in [INAUDIBLE] California.
I don't remember at the moment the name.
It was called the liberty ships.
I think my ship was-- no, it was-- I don't remember now.
November-- we were 16 days on the boat.
And we arrived November the 2nd to Tacoma, Washington.
It was a new world.
We felt very good to arriving here.
And there were people-- Jewish people committee-- came out to greet us.
[INAUDIBLE] First we passed-- we went through Canada-- Victoria, BC, a nice little beautiful town about 60,000.
Some Jewish people came there to greet us just to say hello.
But in Tacoma, some people were-- [INAUDIBLE] were too [? far from there?] so they took us into-- to Seattle, Washington so far to a-- to a Mr.
Gnute.
Was their name.
And there was a [NON-ENGLISH] there-- a very nice aristocratic family.
And feeded us with a supper.
And then we went on a-- on the train a few days [INAUDIBLE] and arrived in Philadelphia [? on a Friday ?] to my cousin, which I never had met before.
And they treated me very nicely.
I was there from Friday to a week later Saturday night.
There was a convention-- a [INAUDIBLE] convention.
Then [? suddenly ?] I went to Brooklyn, and I slept over really, in a-- I think there was a YMCH.
A [INAUDIBLE].
I slept all--
The YMHA.
The YM-- no, I think it was--
See, I think so.
I'm not sure.
Yeah.
I think it cost me a dollar.
And that was it, and then I started to-- to-- this was another chapter which there's nothing dramatic much about it that has to do with-- with-- with this-- all this Holocaust.
But here, I straggle up.
And I-- in 1956, January, we got married.
My wife.
[LAUGHS]
Her name is Claire.
Her maiden name was Skolnick and her father was a cantor.
She was born in Philadelphia already here.
Oh, a funny thing if you're interested.
There was a guy who was a very close friend of mine.
We were friends in Shanghai, and then Japan, and then Lithuania as refugees.
And he went-- came here and went to Canada.
He settled in Canada.
So he and I-- once he comes a year.
And we are still two single guys.
And we meet and-- oh, oh.
[INAUDIBLE] What are you [? come? ?] He says, I came for a [INAUDIBLE].
I'm getting older.
I'm getting old and it's no longer a joke.
I want a [INAUDIBLE].
OK, [INAUDIBLE].
Will you?
He said somebody [INAUDIBLE], and I'm supposed to meet the girl.
OK.
So then he comes.
I met the girl.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE].
He says-- [? nothing ?] [? to ?] her.
I say why?
He says, she's-- she's so nice.
And she's cultured.
And she-- you know, he [INAUDIBLE]-- he was a teacher at home yet.
And she [INAUDIBLE] speaks good Hebrew, and she's so smart.
You know. ?] I should show him now.
[LAUGHS] And she-- she looks good and nothing will happen.
I says why?
And I keep on-- [INAUDIBLE] you-- we-- we had a nickname for him-- [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS] Just because he was smart and always on the trigger.
[INAUDIBLE] He was like [INAUDIBLE].
I says what?
And [?
I'd ?] used to give him, what do you want?
The Marilyn Monroe?
Or Elizabeth Taylor?
This is what you're looking for?
Look at you.
Who are you?
Who-- what are you looking for?
He says this is not the point.
Not a Marilyn Monroe, not Elizabeth Taylor.
She is so-- he told me-- so many years younger than me.
And I'm so short.
He's a really short guy.
And so I says, try.
You know how the girls are.
She said not to call her.
I say you know how the girls are-- try and call her.
It is because of this he wouldn't call her.
I said, so what is the problem?
He says she is a few years younger-- quite so many years younger.
And I'm so short, and she's taller than me.
I say if this is the truth-- and he had a full mouth of Jews about her-- I says look, if you're sure you're [INAUDIBLE], if it's so good, give me the telephone number, and I'll call her.
He says why not?
How old were you at this time?
He gave me the telephone number, and here we are.
And I call her up and then a funny thing happened.
I had distance cousin that I used to be in their house.
They were very nice to me and Rabbi Frankel.
And he had a daughter-- a teenager.
And she was always trying to fix me up.
[INAUDIBLE]
So one day she-- this-- this teenager daughter-- had a friend in the same house, living in the same house where my wife lived with her parents.
One day, after I had taken her out already, one day this girl comes and asks me to [INAUDIBLE] with the same girl.
[LAUGHS] And that's how it was.
What was your wedding like?
Very lively and a lot of people that I didn't invite, because it was right after the war.
And then I am from a rabbinical house and there were some [INAUDIBLE] from the [NON-ENGLISH] that heard I'm getting married.
They came to [? run. ?] It was a very-- it was touching too, but there was a-- a lot of people unexpected came.
And--
And--
Slowly.
Once I got a letter.
Still being in Shanghai, I got a letter from a couple of people who survived.
And one of them just called me on the telephone-- writing me that-- that they know that my-- my-- my parents got killed, my father and mother got killed.
So I was in the house crying for a few weeks.
And, you know, you talk to people.
You see lists in the papers.
My father-- I still don't know where and how he got killed.
No.
Only me.
No.
Uncles, aunts, you know, no.
My grandfather had six children-- four sons and two daughters also.
One daughter had about 11 children in [?
Vokvill ?] where the ghetto was-- all killed out in one day.
No one or two, but before that.
And one after the other taken like-- like-- like chickens.
The older one, when he was about to [INAUDIBLE] started to [INAUDIBLE].
I know you're going to kill me.
Don't forget you're going to have [INAUDIBLE], and they shot him-- everybody.
Everybody [INAUDIBLE].
I came to New York right after-- I was eight days in-- in Philadelphia and then I came to New York.
I did so many things.
I can't begin to tell you.
I was in the beginning selling vacuum cleaners door to door, I think.
Then I was-- oh, I was a-- I was a Hebrew teacher in Rockaway Beach.
I came to-- the first call was-- was from the guy that his mother arrived to-- to New York also from Shanghai.
I went to visit them, and they had papers to come to New York from Rockaway Beach from a shul that they accepted as rabbi.
That he-- he didn't know more than davening.
So I was there in the hotel when they arrived in the Hotel [?
Marysas ?] in Manhattan, 101st Street, I think.
There was the president from that shul telling them that he can't give him a rabbi position.
That man couldn't no more than talk, more than daven.
But he can give him a Hebrew teaching position.
He was so far from that.
So talking, talking-- he says no.
And he says yes.
He didn't realize the president was a typical American, didn't realize that somebody sent his papers as a rabbi wouldn't know more than a little daven.
And they told him.
Look, now here what he tells-- I says how about me?
If he doesn't want-- I'll [? go. ?] Anyways, to make a story short, he gave me an interview.
I went to the interview.
I never thought I-- in my life.
But when I stood there with this Rabbi Frankel.
She had a Hebrew class.
And she had also a sister coming in.
She was teaching.
So for a day or two-- a few days that I had until the interview-- they gave me a little low down.
And then I took a English-Hebrew dictionary.
Anyway, I went to the interview, and I was accepted.
And I was there a few years as a Hebrew teacher.
Then I taught the kid the wrong [INAUDIBLE] from another week.
[LAUGHS] So I was fired after that.
I was-- I went-- I had a store in Miami, a Jewish bookstore in Miami, who a friend of mine who was working for a Hebrew publishing company here.
And this was all over the country used to buy there.
He found out that there was a shortage in [INAUDIBLE] there and other things.
So he decided he had the money that he'd open a store.
He sent me to open a store in Miami Beach.
I was there a few months.
And then he sent somebody else.
I couldn't stay.
It was back to New York, and I did different things all the time.
And then when I got-- I went in-- after I got married-- no, no, as a single man, I was for years managing a nursing home.
This was quite a number of years from one to the other.
It was with that family-- with that Frankel family.
I was quite a number of years there, even when I got married for a few years I got-- I was in managing homes.
And I made a living, and my wife was teaching-- a Hebrew teacher.
Then-- then she went into the public school system for quite a number of years later.
And then I bought a small nursing home in [?
Pecha, ?] Long Island.
And it was-- turned out bad because of some local things with Welfare Department, with stealing.
It was bad for the-- for the homes.
It wasn't enough.
So I didn't have enough money to make it [INAUDIBLE] and this little time.
I was out of debt-- little things back and forth, back and forth.
